Section: ABSTRACT
Page: 1
Block type: text

Atopic dermatitis (AD) is a common chronic inflammatory skin condition that significantly impairs patients' quality of life as a result of intense  itching  and  persistent  eczematous lesions. Although AD has a multifaceted etiology-including genetic predisposition, environmental triggers, barrier dysfunction, and dysregulated immune responses-interleukin-4 (IL-4) has a recognized central role in its pathogenesis. This narrative review explores the role of IL-4 in

In addition to symptomatic relief, suppressing IL-4 signaling may also offer potential for disease modification, altering AD's progression and possibly preventing the onset of other atopic conditions. This review highlights the crucial role of IL-4 as a therapeutic target in AD. By understanding the role of IL-4 in AD pathogenesis and exploring the therapeutic implications of targeting IL-4 pathways, this work can contribute to guide future research concerning treatment approaches and also emphasize the need for early and targeted interventions to mitigate disease impact and ultimately improve patient quality of life.

Section: Key Summary Points
Page: 2
Block type: text

Interleukin-4 (IL-4) plays a crucial role in the pathogenesis of atopic dermatitis (AD), as it contributes to skin barrier dysfunction, pruritus, dysbiosis, and inflammation, which are the four cardinal pillars of the condition.
IL-4 is implicated in the pathogenesis of other atopic conditions such as asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergies, making it a key player in the atopic march and underscoring its importance beyond AD.
Targeting IL-4/IL-13 signaling, particularly with targeted therapies like dupilumab, has revolutionized AD management, leading to significant improvements in patient symptoms, quality of life, and overall disease control.
Dupilumab has shown some potential to impact disease modification, with the possibility of altering AD progression and preventing the development of other allergic diseases in certain populations when introduced early.

Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: INTRODUCTION
Page: 2
Block type: text

Atopic dermatitis (AD), also known as atopic eczema, is a chronic inflammatory skin disease that causes intense itching and recurrent eczematous lesions [1]. Characterized by debilitating signs and symptoms-intense itching (pruritus), eczematous lesions, and impaired skin barrierAD disrupts physical health and significantly impacts patients' emotional and psychological well-being, often requiring long-term management and affecting overall quality of life [2]. Globally, AD prevalence has been increasing, affecting 3-10% of adults and 10-20% of children [3], with notable regional variations. This underscores the need for a better understanding of the underlying mechanisms of AD and the development of more effective treatment strategies [4-6].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: INTRODUCTION
Page: 2
Block type: text

The precise etiology of AD is unclear, but a growing body of evidence implicates a complex interplay of genetic predisposition, environmental triggers, epidermal barrier dysfunction, dysbiosis, and dysregulated immune responses [7]. Central to this process is the role of interleukin-4 (IL-4), a cytokine that drives T helper 2 (Th2)  cell-mediated  pathways,  exacerbating inflammation, barrier disruption, and other characteristics of AD [8]. Beyond its direct role in AD, the effects of IL-4 extend to other atopic conditions such as asthma and allergic rhinitis, highlighting its broader significance in atopic comorbidities [9-11].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: INTRODUCTION
Page: 2
Block type: text

In recent years, significant progress has been made in understanding the therapeutic implications of IL-4 inhibition. Clinical and preclinical studies have shown promising outcomes, suggesting that targeting IL-4 can lead to substantial improvements in disease signs and symptoms and overall quality of life for patients with AD [12, 13]. This comprehensive review aims to explore the multifaceted role of IL-4 in AD, focusing on its involvement in several mechanisms known to be hallmarks of AD, namely epidermal barrier disruption, chronic inflammation, pruritus, dysbiosis, and immunoglobulin E (IgE) production. Additionally, the impact of IL-4 inhibition will be discussed, including its  potential for disease modification and its significance in the context of other therapeutic approaches. By understanding the critical role of IL-4, this review seeks to understand its role in AD and how targeting the IL-4 pathways can enlighten further treatment approaches.

Section: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 3
Block type: text

The pathogenesis of AD involves a complex interplay of genetic susceptibility, epidermal barrier disruption, immune dysregulation, and skin dysbiosis [6, 14, 15]. Skin barrier dysfunction has been traditionally considered the first step for the onset of AD and the subsequent development of the atopic march [16]. Some patients with AD have an underlying genetic predisposition, mainly mutations in the filaggrin ( FLG ) gene, which encodes a critical structural protein in the stratum corneum involved in maintaining skin barrier integrity. FLG mutations, particularly homozygous, are associated with an increased risk of severe AD with earlier onset, longer persistence, and skin infection, and they occur in 50% of children with moderate to severe AD [17-19]. Filaggrin deficiency leads to impaired skin barrier function, characterized by increased transepidermal water loss and decreased structural stability of the skin. This compromised barrier facilitates the penetration of irritants, allergens, and colonization by microbes, particularly Staphylococcus aureus [15, 20-22].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 3
Block type: text

While FLG mutations have been the most studied, other epidermal barrier genes-loricrin ( LOR ), filaggrin-2 ( FLG-2 ), hornerin ( HRNR ), SPINK5 , SPRR3 , desmoglein 1 ( DSG1 ), involucrin ( IVL ), and claudin-1 ( CLDN1 )-as well as immune system-related genes-Toll-like receptor 2 ( TLR2 ), TLR4 , TLR9 , nucleotide-binding oligomerization domain-containing protein 1
( NOD1 ), NOD2 , and interferon-gamma ( IFN γ ) -have been associated with AD. The prevalence of these genetic variants differs across populations, highlighting the complex and heterogeneous nature of AD [19, 23].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 3
Block type: text

Upon disruption of the skin barrier, keratinocytes, the predominant cells in the epidermis, release various immune-regulating molecules called alarmins, such as IL-1 β (IL-1 β ),  IL-25, IL-33,  and  thymic  stromal  lymphopoietin (TSLP). These molecules play a crucial role in activating  the  immune  system,

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 3
Block type: text

particularly Th2, Th17, and Th22 immune responses [15, 16]. Th2 cells release cytokines like IL-4 and IL-13, which further weaken the skin barrier by downregulating the expression of essential proteins like filaggrin. Additionally, TSLP, IL-25 and IL-33 promote innate lymphoid cells type 2 (ILC2) expansion, which leads to the production of IL-4, IL-13, IL-9, and IL-5, further impairing skin barrier [24]. IL-4, IL-13, and IL-31 also affect keratinocytes' differentiation and trigger increased pruritus [25]. This creates a vicious cycle of barrier dysfunction and inflammation, perpetuating the disease state [21, 22]. Indeed, the  progression  from  acute  to  chronic

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 3
Block type: text

AD involves not only a quantitative intensification of the Th2, Th22, and Th17 responses but also a notable upregulation of Th1-associated genes, resulting in increased secretion of IFN γ and IL-2 [26, 27]. However, the relative importance of these subtypes of T cells can vary according to ethnicity [28].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 3
Block type: text

The itch-scratch cycle is another key component of AD pathophysiology. The release of Th2 cytokines such as TSLP, IL-2, IL-4, IL-13, and IL-31 combined with amplified sensory nerve signals intensifies  itching  [1,  21,  29]. Indeed, IL-31 has been recently identified as a key player in pruritus, along with IL-4 that can signal directly through receptors expressed on various branches of epidermal and dermal nerve endings [21]. Contrary to earlier assumptions, histaminergic pathways play a less significant role in AD, with histamine from mast cells contributing minimally to the itch-scratch cycle. The sensation of itch induces scratching, which further damages the skin barrier, leading to more inflammation and a heightened immune response.

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 4
Block type: text

Skin dysbiosis is an important hallmark of AD [1]. As a result of impaired epidermal barrier function and dysregulated antimicrobial immunity, the skin of patients with AD is highly susceptible to bacterial infections, particularly from S. aureus [6, 30]. The skin microbiome of patients with AD is marked by reduced diversity of bacterial strains and increased colonization of S. aureus , which is present on the skin of 60-100% of those affected, comparing with 5-30% of healthy subjects [6]. S. aureus contributes to AD pathogenesis in several ways, including further barrier disruption with the production of ceramidases and direct proinflammatory effects such as type 2 immune responses [1]. Additionally, other coagulase-negative staphylococcus strains, Staphylococcus epidermidis and Staphylococcus hominis , are increased in AD skin [30].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 4
Block type: text

Recently, gut dysbiosis has also been linked to AD pathogenesis [31]. It results from an imbalance in gut microbiota that leads to immune dysregulation and barrier disruption, allowing inflammatory substances to permeate the bloodstream and reach the skin. This exacerbates the Th2 immune response and contributes to tissue damage and sensitization to these chemicals. Moreover, dietary antigens, bacterial toxins, and pathogens may accumulate in the skin, further impairing skin barrier and perpetuating inflammation [31-33]. This systemic aspect of AD illustrates the interconnectedness of different body systems in the disease pathogenesis [34, 35].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: THE ROLE OF IL-4 IN THE FOUR CARDINAL PILLARS OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 4
Block type: text

IL-4 plays a pivotal role in the pathophysiology of AD, influencing each of the four cardinal pillars of AD: pruritus, inflammation, skin barrier dysfunction, and dysbiosis (Fig. 1).

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: THE ROLE OF IL-4 IN THE FOUR CARDINAL PILLARS OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 4
Block type: text

IL-4  signals  through  two  different  receptor complexes: a type I receptor composed of IL-4R α chain and common γ chain (IL-4 R γ subunit), and a type II receptor consisting of IL-4R α and IL-13R α 1 subunits. The type I receptor specifically transduces signals for IL-4 and it is primarily expressed on hematopoietic cells, such as T cells. In contrast, the type II receptor can be activated by either IL-4 or IL-13. This receptor is expressed on a broader range of cells, including non-hematopoietic cells such as fibroblasts, keratinocytes, and other epithelial cells, which are key players in tissue inflammation and remodeling. While both IL-4 and IL-13 can signal through the type II receptor, the effects of IL-13 are restricted to cells expressing this receptor, which explains why IL-13 may not induce Th2 differentiation directly-as T cells express the type I receptor but not the type II receptor [36-38].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: THE ROLE OF IL-4 IN THE FOUR CARDINAL PILLARS OF ATOPIC DERMATITIS
Page: 4
Block type: text

In the development of allergic inflammation, IL-4 and IL-13 have overlapping and additive roles [38]. IL-4 promotes the development of Th2 cells, while IL-13 is critical in promoting tissue  inflammation,  acting  on  non-hematopoietic cells through the type II receptor. IL-4 is involved in Th2 differentiation and controls lymphocyte functions such as IgE synthesis in B cells [39, 40]. Hence, IL-4 has a more central action in AD, as it acts in the lymph nodes, whereas IL-13 plays a more peripheral role, exerting its effects mainly in the skin. Th2 cells, ILC2, mast cells, eosinophils, and basophils all produce both IL-4 and IL-13 [38, 40].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Skin Barrier Dysfunction
Page: 4
Block type: text

IL-4 significantly impairs skin barrier function, a  hallmark  of  AD.  IL-4  stimulation  in  vitro changes early and terminal keratinocytes differentiation, suppressing the expression of both terminal keratinocyte differentiation proteins (i.e.,  filaggrin,  loricrin,  and  involucrin)  and genes related to differentiating keratinocytes at early stages. The downregulated proteins are critical for maintaining the integrity of the skin barrier, leading to the disruption of tight junctions and impaired ceramide production in the skin [39, 41-43]. Consequently, this causes increased transepidermal water loss, which results in a compromised barrier that is more susceptible to irritants, allergens, and microbial invasion [15, 22, 42, 43].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Skin Barrier Dysfunction
Page: 4
Block type: text

Furthermore, IL-4 alters lipid metabolism and content in the skin by reducing the production of lipids like ceramides crucial for barrier function. Patients with AD exhibit reduced levels of total ceramide and free fatty acids [44, 45]. In particular, larger species of ceramides (> 40 total carbons, like Cer[NH], Cer[EOS], Cer[NP], Cer[EOH], and Cer[EOP]), which are essential for the formation of a tightly packed lipid barrier are expressed at significantly lower levels, whereas smaller ceramide species (<  40 total carbons), that render the skin more permeable, are expressed at higher levels [45]. IL-4 and IL-13 were shown to downregulate the expression of lipid chain elongases 3 and 6 (ELOVL3, ELOVL6) in  keratinocytes,  highlighting  the

Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Skin Barrier Dysfunction
Page: 4
Block type: text

crosstalk between inflammatory response and skin barrier components [45]. In addition, the deficient lipid composition that characterizes patients with AD correlates with altered expression of other enzymes also involved in ceramide and fatty acids biosynthesis, like β -glucocerebrosidase
Fig. 1 The role of IL-4 in the four cardinal pillars of atopic dermatitis: skin barrier dysfunction, skin dysbiosis, inflammation, and pruritus
(GBA), acid-sphingomyelinase (aSmase), ceramide synthase 3 (CerS3), stearoyl CoA desaturase (SCD), and ELOVL1 [46]. This lipid deficiency further exacerbates barrier dysfunction and facilitates the chronic inflammatory state observed in AD [42, 47].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Skin Dysbiosis
Page: 5
Block type: text

Concerning the skin microbiome, IL-4 has been linked to increased S. aureus adhesion and penetration into the epidermis, which correlates not only with elevated levels of IL-4 but also other cytokines (IL-13, IL-22, and TSLP), along with decreased expression of antimicrobial peptides (AMPs), critical for maintaining skin integrity and microbial balance [48]. Specifically, IL-4 attenuates AMP production by suppressing upregulation of beta defensins
(HBD-2 and HBD-3), lipocalin-2, and LL-37, even antagonizing IL-17-induced AMP production in keratinocytes, which further compromises the skin's defensive capabilities [41].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Skin Dysbiosis
Page: 6
Block type: text

Moreover, IL-4 promotes fibroblast production of extracellular matrix components like collagen, fibronectin, and fibrinogen, which enhance S. aureus adhesion to the skin [41]. This interaction underscores the contribution of IL-4 to both skin barrier dysfunction and increased susceptibility to bacterial infections. Consequently, this dysregulation contributes to an environment that is not only more susceptible to bacterial infections but also exacerbates the overall inflammatory response associated with AD.

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Pruritus
Page: 6
Block type: text

IL-4  is  paramount  for  chronic  itch  in  AD through multiple direct and indirect mechanisms. By acting on the IL-4R α expressed on the surface of the peripheral sensory neurons, IL-4 promotes their direct activation, releasing different pruritogenic mediators and increasing their responsiveness to subthreshold levels of several pruritogens [41, 49-51].
Furthermore,  IL-4  amplifies  the  role  of IL-31 in pruritus by enhancing the interaction  between  this  critical  cytokine  and  its receptor (IL-31R α ). This interaction leads to increased production of CCL17 and CCL22. IL-4 also upregulates the expression of IL-31R α on sensory neurons, further exacerbating the itch response. This persistent itching not only leads to further skin damage through scratching but also significantly impacts the quality of life of patients with AD [52].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Inflammation
Page: 6
Block type: text

IL-4 is a central player in both the chronic and acute inflammatory processes of AD. IL-4 reduces dendritic cells' expression of IL-12, MHC class II, and costimulatory molecules, while increasing IL-10 production. This favors the differentiation of naïve   CD4 +  and   CD8 + T cells toward type 2 cells. Additionally, IL-4
stimulates the expression of chemokine C-C motif ligand (CCL) CCL26 in keratinocytes and CCL11 in fibroblasts, which are critical for eosinophil recruitment [41, 53]. The recruitment of eosinophils, along with other inflammatory cells like mast cells, causes the release of pro-inflammatory mediators that sustain chronic inflammation in the skin. In addition, IL-4 supports the development of ILC2 cells, creating a positive feedback loop that enhances the Th2 response [16, 41, 43]. IL-4 also inhibits the function of regulatory T cells (Tregs), which are crucial for maintaining immune tolerance and regulating inflammatory responses [14, 54].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Inflammation
Page: 6
Block type: text

IL-4 is a key driver of IgE production, a relevant component of the AD response [55]. It promotes class switching in B cells, leading to the production of IgE antibodies. These antibodies bind to mast cells and basophils, sensitizing them to allergens. Upon exposure to allergens, these sensitized cells release inflammatory mediators, contributing to the inflammation characteristic of AD [41, 56, 57]. In cases of IgE-allergic AD, the binding of IgE to receptors on dendritic cells, like Langerhans cells and inflammatory dendritic epidermal cells, enhances allergen uptake and initiates hypersensitivity reactions [58]. The elevated levels of IgE not only perpetuate the allergic response but also exacerbate skin inflammation and barrier dysfunction, creating a feedback loop that sustains the disease [14, 41]. In fact, the reduction in IgE levels during treatment with dupilumab appears to correlate with a decrease in AD flares, emphasizing its key role in the disease's pathogenesis [59].
Table 1 highlights the multifaceted role of IL-4 in AD.

Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: THE EXPANSIVE ROLE OF IL-4 IN THE ATOPIC MARCH
Page: 6
Block type: text

AD is often associated with other atopic conditions, such as asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergies, known as atopic comorbidities [62]. The term 'atopic march' refers to the typical progression of these allergic diseases, often  beginning  with  AD  and  progressing sequentially, while subsequently increasing the likelihood of developing the others and amplifying the allergic burden [63]. While the concept of atopic march is well established, recent research suggests that this progression does not follow a linear path for all individuals, giving rise to the idea of 'atopic trajectories' to describe the various pathways through which allergic conditions can develop over time [64, 65].

Section: THE EXPANSIVE ROLE OF IL-4 IN THE ATOPIC MARCH
Page: 8

IL-4 plays a central role in the pathogenesis of the atopic comorbidities by promoting allergic  inflammation  and  IgE  production, whose contribution is more important in these comorbidities.  The  involvement  of  IL-4  in these conditions underscores its expansive role in the atopic march, highlighting the need for targeted therapies that address IL-4-mediated pathways across different atopic diseases [66].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Asthma
Page: 8
Block type: text

Approximately one-third of patients with AD develop asthma. IL-4 plays a significant role in asthma, as evidenced by the increase of IL-4R α expression  in  bronchial  biopsy  specimens from atopic and non-atopic asthmatic subjects
[67]. Besides, IL-4 induces IgE isotype switch, increases expression of vascular cell adhesion molecule 1 (VCAM-1), promotes eosinophil transmigration, stimulates mucus production, and aids in the differentiation of Th2 lymphocytes, leading to cytokine release [68]. While its role often overlaps with IL-13, IL-4 also acts independently. Evidence suggests that IL-4 exerts a superior influence, since the inhibition of both cytokines with dupilumab has shown benefits in moderate to severe asthma-by reducing exacerbations, and symptoms and improving quality of life-while single inhibition of IL-13 by lebrikizumab and tralokinumab did not induce significant improvements [69, 70].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Rhinitis
Page: 8
Block type: text

Many patients with AD frequently suffer from allergic and non-allergic rhinitis. IL-4 is overexpressed in allergic rhinitis patients, which drives the allergic response, particularly through IgE production [9, 71]. Recent data indicated that inhibition of IL-4 and IL-13 with dupilumab reduced symptom burden in allergic rhinitis, particularly by suppression of IgE production [72]. In contrast, targeting only IL-13 has not reached the expected results [9].

Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Food allergy
Page: 9
Block type: text

A notable proportion of adult patients with AD have food sensitivity (28.6%) and food allergy (24.1%), with prevalence rates even higher in children [62]. A recent meta-analysis described a pooled prevalence of 40.7% for challengedproven food allergy in patients, ranging from 34.1% to 47.5% [73]. IL-4 is implicated in this condition by regulating Th2 cell proliferation and survival and IgE synthesis, leading to disruption of the gut barrier and increased permeability of these allergens. Recent findings showed that dupilumab induced a significant decrease in food-specific IgE levels in children with AD, reinforcing the role of the IL-4/IL-13 axis in food allergies [74].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Inhibition of IL-4: Lessons from In vitro studies and Animal Models
Page: 9
Block type: text

The inhibition of IL-4R α has been a focal point of  therapeutic  research  in  allergic  diseases, including AD. Several in vitro and in vivo studies show the potential of IL-4R antagonism in modulating allergic responses central to these conditions [51].
In vitro studies showed that IL-4R blocking reduces IL-4-activities, such as B cell and T cell proliferation and differentiation into Th2 cells [75, 76]. Consequently, it decreases B cell activities like IgG1 and IgE secretion [77]. Moreover, suppression of IgE production was corroborated by  a  study  with  an  IL-4R  antagonist  which inhibited IL-4- and IL-13-induced STAT6 phosphorylation [78].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Inhibition of IL-4: Lessons from In vitro studies and Animal Models
Page: 9
Block type: text

Inhibiting IL-4R in animal models has demonstrated  considerable  effects  in  mitigating allergic symptoms. Early research revealed that soluble IL-4R blocked IgE secretion up to 85% by neutralizing endogenous IL-4. This finding highlighted the receptor's potential not only as an antagonist but also as modulator of cytokine delivery in therapeutic contexts [79]. In mice models of asthma-characterized by irregular tissue structure and severe inflammation-blocking IL-4R resulted in more regular tissue structure and light inflammation, due to inhibited IL-4/STAT6 pathway [80]. Furthermore, IL-4R α antagonists were shown to inhibit allergeninduced airway eosinophilia and hyperresponsiveness in murine models [78].
Altogether,  these  studies  highlighted  the potential of IL-4 signaling suppression in treating atopic conditions. Preclinical models demonstrated that IL-4R α antagonism effectively reduces inflammation and IgE production, laying the foundation for biologic therapies targeting diseases like AD.

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: The Rise of Targeted Therapies
Page: 9
Block type: text

The rise of targeted therapies-biologic and non-biologic agents-marks a significant shift in the management of AD and other allergic disorders, offering hope for long-term disease control and better patient outcomes [81]. Historically, AD treatment has relied in topical and oral corticosteroids and other immunosuppressive medications. However, with the increasing understanding of the complex pathophysiology of AD, more precise targeted therapies have been developed [82, 83].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: The Rise of Targeted Therapies
Page: 9
Block type: text

Among the less targeted therapies are janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors. These small molecules target multiple pathways involved in AD by blocking  the  activity  of  the  JAK  family  of enzymes (JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, and TYK2), subsequently disrupting cytokine signaling, particularly IL-4, IL-13, and IL-31 [81]. This broader inhibition allows JAK inhibitors to modulate a wide range of immune responses; however, their less selective nature compared to biologics also increases the risk of off-target effects and associated side effects [84, 85]. Nonetheless, JAK inhibitors like abrocitinib, upadacitinib, and baricitinib have shown efficacy and tolerability, making them a valuable option for certain patient populations [86].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: The Rise of Targeted Therapies
Page: 9
Block type: text

In  contrast,  biologic  therapies  directed  at the IL-4/IL-13 pathway offer a more targeted approach to AD management, by inhibiting two of the key cytokines to the disease process. As a result of the established importance of IL-4 in AD, therapies that block IL-4R α have attracted significant  interest.  The  breakthrough  came with dupilumab, the first biologic approved in 2017 for moderate to severe AD, which significantly improved treatment outcomes by inhibiting both IL-4 and IL-13 signaling through their shared receptor (IL-4R α ).

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: The Rise of Targeted Therapies
Page: 10
Block type: text

Dupilumab, a fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody (mAb), has demonstrated significant improvements in disease outcomes in clinical trials, reducing symptoms such as pruritus, anxiety, and depression, while also improving skin clearance and overall quality of life. These benefits have been sustained over time, even in real-world settings, with a favorable safety profile [87-89]. The most frequent side effects were ocular conditions, particularly conjunctivitis, observed in up to 28% of dupilumab users in clinical trials and up to 38.2% in real-world settings [14]. Other reported adverse events in patients treated with dupilumab were nasopharyngitis, headache, injection site reaction, and an increase in oral herpes infections [90].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: The Rise of Targeted Therapies
Page: 10
Block type: text

Dupilumab has also been evaluated in children and adolescents, and the efficacy and safety appear to be similar [14, 91]. Trials in children aged 6 months to 6 years and 6 to 12 years with severe AD reported significant improvements, with mostly transient mild to moderate adverse events [92, 93]. A phase 3 trial in the same age group also reported significant improvements in  symptoms  and  quality  of  life,  although dupilumab was administered alongside topical corticosteroids [94].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: The Rise of Targeted Therapies
Page: 10
Block type: text

The success of dupilumab has sparked the development of other monoclonal antibodies targeting the IL-4/IL-13 axis. Rademikibart (CBP-201),  directed  at  IL-4R α ,  has  shown promise in clinical trials by suppressing Th2driven inflammation, similarly to dupilumab, and significantly decreasing EASI and pruritus, with a favorable safety profile [95, 96]. Manfidokimab (AK120), also directed at IL-4R α , showed preliminary efficacy and an acceptable safety profile [97]. Another example is eblasakimab (ASLAN004), a first-in-class, fully human mAb that prevents IL-4/IL-13 axis signaling by binding to the IL-13R α 1 subunit of the type 2 receptors. This does not completely inhibit IL-4 signaling, resulting in a partial modulation of IL-4 effects, while still significantly  impacting  the  overall  inflammatory response [98].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: The Rise of Targeted Therapies
Page: 10
Block type: text

Therapies selectively  targeting  IL-13  and without interfering with IL-4 signaling have also been developed. Lebrikizumab, recently approved for use in patients  over  12  years old,  impairs  the  heterodimerization  of  the IL-4R α -IL-13R α 1  signaling  complex,  thus blocking only IL-13 signaling. Phase 3 trials reported improved skin clearance, itch, and sleep in patients, with a favorable safety profile [99, 100]. Additionally, tralokinumab, a fully human mAb which binds to IL-13 and blocks its interaction with the receptor, has shown significant improvements in AD compared with placebo, but it was associated with more injection site reactions, compared to dupilumab [81,  101,  102].  While  these  IL-13-targeting therapies have demonstrated improvements in  AD signs and symptoms, they were also associated with some adverse events, including conjunctivitis and injection site reactions. Although these therapies are beneficial, they may not fully address the broader immune dysregulation seen in AD compared to IL-4/IL-13 inhibition.

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: The Rise of Targeted Therapies
Page: 10
Block type: text

Treating AD by targeting either the IL-4/ IL-13 axis or IL-13 alone has proven effective, but the central role of IL-4 in AD cannot be overlooked. IL-4 is critical in Th2 cell differentiation, IgE class switching, and the promotion of chronic pruritus makes it a more comprehensive target for addressing the immune dysregulation underlining AD. Unlike IL-13, which predominantly acts at the peripheral level, particularly in the skin, IL-4 is essential for driving systemic immune responses, making it a central player in both the immune system's central processes and peripheral tissue inflammation. Table 2 shows an overview of the differential roles of these two cytokines in AD.

Section: Can IL-4 Inhibition Promote Disease Modification?
Page: 11
Block type: text

Disease modification in AD refers to interventions that can alter the natural course of the disease, potentially preventing or delaying the progression to more severe forms and associated comorbidities [103]. This is particularly relevant for the atopic march, the progression from AD in early childhood to the development of other atopic conditions like asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergies in adulthood [104].
In AD, the atopic march or atopic trajectories  only  occur  in  a  subset  of  patientsapproximately two-thirds-with the risk being higher in children who produce IgE antibodies in response to environmental triggers [63, 105]. By blocking both IL-4 and IL-13 signaling, dupilumab has shown significant potential for disease modification in AD. Treatment with dupilumab has demonstrated improvements in disease severity, while also reducing serum total IgE levels in adolescents and adults [63]. This suggests that by targeting the key cytokines driving  the

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Can IL-4 Inhibition Promote Disease Modification?
Page: 11
Block type: text

Th2-mediated inflammation in AD, it may be possible to slow or even prevent the progression to other atopic conditions. A meta-analysis based on data from clinical trials documented a 34% reduction in new allergies in patients over 12 years old, an effect that persisted even during off-treatment periods. This meta-analysis supported that dupilumab may have potential for disease modification, particularly in adolescents, patients with early disease onset and with high baseline IgE levels. It is still unknown if the effect is due to the combined inhibition of IL-4/IL-13 or to IL-4 or IL-13 alone, as there are no similar data available with selective IL-13 inhibitors. However, on the basis of the current knowledge of IL-4, its inhibition appears to be crucial for the pathogenesis of allergic comorbidities. Future studies are required to explore this topic further and also because real-world evidence is scarce, particularly in children [63].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Can IL-4 Inhibition Promote Disease Modification?
Page: 12
Block type: text

A recent real-world study in pediatric patients demonstrated that those on dupilumab had a significantly decreased risk of developing atopic march towards asthma or allergic rhinitis (32%), compared with patients on conventional therapy [105]. This effect was more pronounced in younger children, emphasizing the need for early intervention to maximize long-term benefits [63, 105]. The best opportunity to intervene for disease modification and to avoid the development of the atopic comorbidities is probably during childhood, when the immune system and skin barrier are still developing. Indeed, beyond the window of opportunity, even with AD remission via dupilumab, the mechanisms driving atopic march may already be active [63, 105, 106].
The disease-modifying potential of IL-4/IL-13 inhibition, as seen with dupilumab, also introduces the possibility of remission of AD after treatment discontinuation. Remission refers to  the  sustained

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Can IL-4 Inhibition Promote Disease Modification?
Page: 12
Block type: text

improvement or resolution of disease symptoms and signs, even after the discontinuation of the therapeutic intervention, which has been demonstrated for up to 40 weeks in adults [107]. An ongoing study presented at 2024 Society for Pediatric Dermatology Annual Meeting documented that approximately one-third of pediatric patients achieved clinical remission, and half of them maintained therapy-free remission [108]. Data from the Bioday registry also emphasizes the sustained effectiveness of dupilumab up to 5 years after discontinuation of the treatment [109]. More comprehensive data on how dupilumab maintains its effects for longer follow-up periods would further strengthen the case for its role in disease modification, which is still unclear. This could have profound implications for the management of AD, potentially allowing for more durable control of the condition and a reduced need for continuous treatment, particularly when intervention is made in childhood [106]. However, it should be noted that, particularly in childhood, spontaneous remission is a possibility and should always be considered, which adds complexity to the interpretation on off-therapy AD remission.

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: Can IL-4 Inhibition Promote Disease Modification?
Page: 12
Block type: text

A question remains whether therapies targeting only IL-13, such as tralokinumab and lebrikizumab, may also have potential diseasemodifying ability. By targeting both cytokines, dupilumab has the potential to block the biological activity of IL-13 as the most important driving force of the skin inflammation, as well as the more centrally acting IL-4. This dual targeting may give dupilumab a greater impact on the overall disease, but additional data is warranted [110].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: FUTURE PERSPECTIVES AND IMPACT ON CLINICAL PRACTICE
Page: 12
Block type: text

The future of AD treatment is set to face significant changes, as a result of the ongoing development of biologic therapies and small molecules that may provide more precise and effective interventions. The successful introduction of dupilumab, which targets the IL-4/IL-13 axis, has paved the way for a new era in AD management [63, 90, 111].
If future studies, particularly in pediatric populations, confirm dupilumab's ability to modify the course of AD and the atopic march, it could become the first disease-modifying treatment for AD. In addition, other emerging therapies, including OX40/OX40L inhibitors, which target earlier stages of the immunologic cascade, may also show disease-modifying potential. Furthermore, the role of broader-acting agents, like JAK inhibitors, in providing similar long-term benefits remains an area of investigation. As the field of advanced therapies expands, future management may focus on combining biologics with small molecules, bispecific antibodies, ensuring maximal efficacy with minimal adverse effects [81].

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: FUTURE PERSPECTIVES AND IMPACT ON CLINICAL PRACTICE
Page: 12
Block type: text

Integrating personalized medicine into AD management presents as an opportunity for more effective treatment strategies. Advances in genomic research and biomarker identification could enable treatments to be tailored according to individual patient profiles [111]. For example, predictive models could be used in future clinical practice to determine the optimum management alternatives for each individual [111, 112]. The implications for clinical practice will be profound, particularly in its potential to modify the disease course rather than simply managing signs and symptoms.

Document: 001_test
Source: resources\001_test.pdf
Section: CONCLUSION
Page: 13
Block type: text

There is robust evidence supporting the crucial role of IL-4 in the pathogenesis of AD, despite the challenges to distinguish its effect from IL-13. This underscores the strong rationale to target IL-4 in the management of AD. Inhibiting specifically IL-4/IL-13 signaling has led to significant improvements in symptoms, quality of life, and overall disease management, unveiling the potential for disease modification-a capability not yet proven. When introduced early in the course of the disease, dupilumab may not only alleviate current symptoms, but also prevent the onset of associated allergic diseases, positioning IL-4 as a central player in altering the disease trajectory.
As we deepen our understanding of this critical cytokine, the potential for novel therapies continues to expand. With ongoing research and innovative approaches, there is hope for more effective and comprehensive management strategies that could redefine AD treatment in the years to come. Such advancements could ultimately improve the quality of life for millions affected by this debilitating condition.

Atopic  dermatitis  (eczema)  in  infants  remains  one  of  the most  prevalent  and  socially  significant  forms  of  chronic inflammatory skin disorders in early childhood. Since 2016, a steady increase in incidence has been observed, particularly in  highly  developed  and  urbanised  regions.  The  disease  is not only prevalent but also often severe, with symptoms such as intense pruritus, sleep disturbances, emotional lability, and chronic  relapse. The aim of this study was to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of a comprehensive treatment approach in   infants with moderate-to-severe eczema complicated by polyvalent allergic sensitisation. The methodology   involved a randomised controlled trial that included 87 children aged 3 to 12 months. Participants were divided into two groups. The experimental group received combined therapy comprising pseudoceramides, a   calcineurin   inhibitor, and an oral synbiotic, while the control group received standard therapy with emollients and   antihistamines. The  results  demonstrated  that  by  the  eighth  week,  patients  in  the  experimental  group  exhibited  a    reduction in    SCORAD  index  of  more  than  60%  (to  21.4  ±  6.5);  a  2.7-fold  decrease  in  pruritus  severity  according  to  the VAS scale (to 2.9±1.3); and a 61% reduction in POEM score (to 7.3). This was accompanied by improved sleep, reduced

irritability,  and  decreased  behavioural  disturbances.  The  control  group  showed  less-pronounced positive   dynamics.   Immunological changes included a significant decrease in total and specific IgE levels, as well as a marked reduction in IL-4 and IL-13 concentrations, indicating attenuation of the Th2-mediated immune response. The prevalence of severe sensitisation decreased by 20%. Microbiota analysis demonstrated increased Bifidobacterium spp.   abundance (+89%; p = 0.011); higher alpha-diversity (Shannon index + 27%; p = 0.004); and reduced E. coli levels (-36%; p = 0.018), suggesting restoration of microbial balance. Thus, this combined therapy demonstrated significant superiority, providing clinical improvement, suppression of the Th2 response, and nor -malisation of gut microbiota in infants with atopic dermatitis and multiple allergic sensitisation.

A  major  aggravating  factor  is  the  presence  of  polyvalent allergic sensitisation, which  complicates  the condition's clinical  presentation  and  limits  the  efficacy  of  standard therapies.  These  developments  underscore  the  need  for novel therapeutic strategies that effectively alleviate both the symptoms  and  the  immune/microbiological  dysregulation associated with the condition.

A distinct challenge in the treatment of infantile eczema lies in the limited efficacy of conventional therapies in cases of  multiple  allergic  sensitisation  [1].  The  use  of  emollients and antihistamines alone in such scenarios yields suboptimal outcomes,  particularly  in  moderate-to-severe  inflammatory presentations. K. Makowska et al. [2] found that monotherapy for atopic dermatitis with polyvalent sensitisation provides only transient symptom relief, with immunopathological processes remaining active, which predisposes patients to early relapse. The authors emphasise that a combined therapeutic approach is warranted in such cases, given the multifactorial nature of the disease's pathogenesis.

Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: NOVEL THERAPEUTIC APPROACHES FOR TREATING MODERATE-TO-SEVERE ATOPIC DERMATITIS IN INFANTS WITH POLYVALENT ALLERGIC SENSITISATION
Page: 2

Inadequate correction of skin barrier dysfunction represents another significant obstacle to effective treatment [3, 4]. Structural abnormalities in the epidermal lipid layer of infants with atopic dermatitis increase allergen penetration and perpetuate chronic inflammation. Research by  E.  Alska  et  al.  [5]  demonstrated  that  in  such  cases, standard  therapies  based  on  conventional  emollients  fail to  sufficiently  restore  skin  barrier  function.  The  authors conclude that the use of components that structurally mimic natural ceramides holds promise for more comprehensive restoration of epidermal homeostasis.

Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: NOVEL THERAPEUTIC APPROACHES FOR TREATING MODERATE-TO-SEVERE ATOPIC DERMATITIS IN INFANTS WITH POLYVALENT ALLERGIC SENSITISATION
Page: 2
Block type: text

The intensity  of  the Th2  immune  response,  and  its  role in  the  pathogenesis  of  eczema  in  infants  with  polyvalent allergic  sensitisation,  also  requires  close  attention. A  study by R. Fornal et al. [6] revealed elevated expression of IL-4 and  IL-13  cytokines  in  this  patient  cohort,  correlating  with patients'  clinical  severity.  However,  attempts  to  modulate this  pathway using systemic immunosuppressants in young children have been limited by the high risk of adverse effects. The authors highlight the need for safer topical agents with immunomodulatory properties.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: NOVEL THERAPEUTIC APPROACHES FOR TREATING MODERATE-TO-SEVERE ATOPIC DERMATITIS IN INFANTS WITH POLYVALENT ALLERGIC SENSITISATION
Page: 2
Block type: text

Disruptions  in  gut  microbiota  represent  a  key  factor  in the  development  and  perpetuation  of  allergic  conditions in  children  [7-9].  According  to  an  analysis  by  J.  Chalmers et  al.  [10],  infants  with  eczema  and  polyvalent  allergic sensitisation  exhibit  reduced  alpha-diversity  of  microbiota, decreased abundance of beneficial Bifidobacterium spp., and an increase in opportunistic pathogens such as Escherichia coli . The authors underscore that restoring microbial balance may serve as a crucial adjunct therapy, particularly through the  use  of  synbiotics,  which  combine  probiotic  strains  and prebiotic substrates.
The  unsatisfactory  dynamics  of  total  and  specific  IgE level  reduction  during  standard  treatment  also  presents  a significant challenge. H.O. Skjerven et al. [11] demonstrated that infants with pronounced sensitisation maintain elevated IgE  levels  even  after  a  course  of  baseline  therapy;  this  is associated with a high risk of disease chronicity. The authors conclude that targeting the immune component of the disease is key to a comprehensive therapeutic approach.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: NOVEL THERAPEUTIC APPROACHES FOR TREATING MODERATE-TO-SEVERE ATOPIC DERMATITIS IN INFANTS WITH POLYVALENT ALLERGIC SENSITISATION
Page: 2
Block type: text

Behavioural disturbances and sleep impairment in infants with severe eczema remain an underappreciated yet significant  factor  worsening  patients'  prognosis.  H.  Kader et  al.  [12]  found  that  persistent  pruritus  and  inflammation lead  to  irritability,  disrupted  sleep  patterns,  and  difficulties in  daily  child  care.  Despite  this,  most  treatment  regimens do not include interventions aimed at rapid symptom relief. The  authors  emphasise  the  importance  of  a  multifaceted, comprehensive approach.
Another  unresolved  issue  is  the  insufficient  duration  of clinical  remission  following  treatment  discontinuation.  The study  by  S.  Sun  et  al.  [13]  showed  that  children  receiving only conventional therapy experienced symptom relapse as early  as  3-4  weeks  after  treatment  cessation.  The  authors highlight  that  effective  therapy  should  not  only  alleviate acute manifestations, but also ensure sustained clinical and immunological improvement.

There is also a need for therapeutic strategies tailored to the specificities of infancy. M. Kelleher et al. [14] emphasise that pharmacokinetic and immunological parameters in infants  under  one  year  of  age  necessitate  a  specialised approach  to  treatment  selection,  as  standard  dosages  and medications used in older children are not always effective or safe in infants. This underscores the importance of developing tailored  treatment  regimens  that  account  for  age-specific characteristics.

Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: NOVEL THERAPEUTIC APPROACHES FOR TREATING MODERATE-TO-SEVERE ATOPIC DERMATITIS IN INFANTS WITH POLYVALENT ALLERGIC SENSITISATION
Page: 2
Block type: text

Given these knowledge gaps and the limitations of existing approaches, there is  a  pressing  need  for  novel  therapeutic strategies  that  provide  multi-component  modulation  of  key pathogenetic  mechanisms  of  the  disease.  Unlike  previous studies, which have focused on isolated interventions, this study evaluates a multicomponent therapeutic regimen  combining  topical  skin  barrier  restoration,  local immunosuppression,  and  oral  synbiotic  supplementation. The  aim  of  this  study  was  to  evaluate  the  clinical  and immunological efficacy of combination therapy in infants with eczema complicated by polyvalent allergic sensitisation. The study  objectives  included  an  analysis  of  skin  manifestation dynamics,  assessment  of  immune  response  parameters, and  evaluation  of  microbiota  status  under  conditions  of comprehensive therapeutic intervention.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Materials and methods
Page: 2
Block type: text

The study was conducted from January to September 2024 at the Department of Dermatology, Medical University of Warsaw, Poland, under both inpatient and outpatient settings. The study enrolled 87 infants (44 boys and 43 girls) aged 3 to 12 months with clinically confirmed diagnoses of moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis based on the diagnostic criteria of J.M. Hanifin and G. Rajka [15] in the presence of concomitant allergic conditions, including  food  sensitisation,  respiratory  hyperreactivity,  and a  positive  family  history  of  atopy.  Patients  with  congenital immunodeficiencies, systemic infections and acute dermatoses, as well as those who had received immunosuppressive therapy within 30 days prior to the study, were excluded. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants' parents.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Materials and methods
Page: 3
Block type: text

Participants were randomised  into two groups.  The experimental  group  (n  =  45)  received  combination  therapy consisting  of  topical  application  of  pseudo-ceramide-based cream (Curel Intensive Moisture Cream, Japan) twice daily; a calcineurin inhibitor (Protopic 0.03%, Japan) once daily on affected areas; and a daily oral dose of 0.3 mL of a synbiotic (Alflorex  Baby  Drops,  Ireland).  The  control  group  (n  =  42) received standard therapy: emollients (Lipikar Baume AP + M,  France)  three  times  daily,  and  an  antihistamine  (Zyrtec, Switzerland)  at  an  age-appropriate  dose  at  bedtime.  The treatment duration  was  eight  weeks.  Both  groups  also received individualised hygiene and dietary recommendations based on allergy testing results.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Materials and methods
Page: 3
Block type: text

Clinical  assessments  were  performed  at  weeks  0,  4,  and 8,  and  the  impact  of  atopic  dermatitis  on  patients'  quality of  life  was  measured using the following validated scales: SCORing  Atopic  Dermatitis  (SCORAD)  for  quantitative evaluation of skin symptom  severity; Visual  Analogue Scale  (VAS)  for self-assessment  of itch intensity  and irritability  levels;  and  Patient-Oriented  Eczema  Measure (POEM). Assessment of allergic sensitisation included skinprick  testing  with  a  standard  allergen  panel  (Stallergenes Greer, France); determination of total and allergenspecific IgE levels in blood serum using an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) with the ImmunoCAP system (Sweden);  and  molecular  allergy  diagnostics  employing the  multiplex  component-based  ALEX²  Allergy  Explorer panel (Austria). To analyse gut microbiota, 16S rRNA gene sequencing  of  faecal  samples  was  performed  using  highthroughput  sequencing  on  the  Illumina  MiSeq  platform (USA). Bioinformatic processing of the obtained data was conducted in the QIIME 2 environment (USA).

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Materials and methods
Page: 3
Block type: text

Additionally, levels of inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-5, IL-13, IL-4) in blood plasma were assessed through multiplex analysis  with  the  Luminex  MAGPIX  platform  (USA).  Blood sampling was performed after overnight fasting during the first and  eighth  study  visits,  and  collected  serum  samples  were stored at -80 °C until analysis. All procedures and interventions were  carried  out  in  accordance  with  the  principles  of  the Declaration  of  Helsinki  and  the  standards  of  Good  Clinical Laboratory Practice (GCLP). The study protocol was reviewed and  approved  by  the  Bioethics  Committee  of  the  National Medical Institute of the Ministry of the Interior and  Administration, Warsaw,  Poland  (Approval  No.    NMIMIA-DER-2023-12).  All procedures were conducted in compliance with the Declaration of  Helsinki.  Written  informed  consent  was  obtained  from  the parents or legal guardians of all participating infants prior to their inclusion in the study.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Materials and methods
Page: 3
Block type: text

Statistical  analysis  of  the  data  was  conducted  using IBM  SPSS  Statistics  v.28  (USA).  The  Shapiro-Wilk  test was  employed  to  assess  the  normality  of  data  distribution. Comparisons  between  groups  were  made  using  Student's t-test for independent samples and the Mann-Whitney U test for cases of non-normal distribution. Intragroup changes were analysed  using  the  paired  t-test.  Differences  in  categorical data were assessed using the χ² test or Fisher's exact test as  appropriate.  Correlation  analysis  was  performed  using Spearman's  rank  correlation  coefficient.  Differences  were considered statistically significant at p < 0.05. The p-value was calculated using Student's t-test for independent samples, as the distribution of SCORAD values at this stage conformed to normality according to the Shapiro-Wilk test.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Results
Page: 3
Block type: text

During the eight-week observation period, marked differences in the dynamics of clinical parameters were observed between the experimental and control groups. At the baseline (week 0), the SCORAD, VAS, and POEM scores were comparable in  both  groups,  confirming  sample  homogeneity  and  the absence of initial  bias.  By  week  4,  the  experimental  group showed  showed  significant  improvement  across  all  clinical parameters, with the most notable decrease observed in the SCORAD index. The positive trend continued through week 8,  when both SCORAD and POEM scores approached the lower range of moderate disease severity, and VAS values indicated a marked reduction in pruritus intensity. The control group  also  demonstrated  improvement,  although  changes were  less  pronounced.  These  differences  were  particularly evident upon analysis of standard deviations and interquartile ranges (Table 1).

A detailed analysis of the dynamics of the SCORAD , VAS, and  POEM  clinical  scales  revealed  significant  differences in  the  efficacy  of  the  applied  therapeutic  approaches  in infants with atopic dermatitis complicated by polyvalent allergic  sensitisation.  At  baseline,  none  of  the  three  scales demonstrated statistically significant differences between the experimental and control groups, indicating initial comparability in  clinical  status,  severity  of  dermatological  symptoms,  and subjective discomfort.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Results
Page: 4
Block type: text

By  the  fourth  week  of  treatment  in  the  experimental group receiving combined therapy -which included pseudoceramides, a calcineurin inhibitor, and an oral synbiotic -  a  marked  reduction  in  the  mean  SCORAD  index  was observed, exceeding 34% compared to baseline. This decline was  accompanied  by  a  statistically  significant  difference from  the  control  group,  where  the  reduction  was  only  16% (p = 0.009), reflecting the moderate efficacy of the standard treatment regimen. By the eighth week, the disparity between the groups had further increased; mean SCORAD values in the experimental group fell within the range characteristic of mild  atopic  dermatitis,  whereas  the  control  group's  scores remained indicative of moderate disease activity. The intergroup  difference  remained  highly  statistically  significant (p < 0.001), confirming the sustained and pronounced effect of the combined therapy.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Results
Page: 4
Block type: text

Concurrently, significant changes were noted in the subjective assessment of pruritus using the VAS scale. While all  participants  initially  reported  itch  severity  scores  of  7-8 points, corresponding to significant discomfort (7-10 points on the VAS scale), by the fourth week, the experimental group's score showed a reduction to 4.3 ± 1.6, indicating moderate pruritus  (4-6  points).  By  the  eighth  week,  the  mean  score further decreased to 2.9 ± 1.3, reflecting a transition to mild symptom severity (1-3 points) or near-complete resolution. The control group also exhibited a reduction in symptom intensity, albeit less pronounced; final scores reached 5.1 ± 1.6, which is still indicative of moderate discomfort. The experimental group also  demonstrated  a  narrower  range  of  individual  scores, suggesting greater uniformity in therapeutic response under the combined intervention.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Results
Page: 4
Block type: text

The POEM scale, which assesses the impact of dermatitis on the child's daily activities and overall condition, revealed a  similar  trend,  further  supporting  the  superiority  of  the experimental protocol, with a 61% reduction in mean scores (from 18.7 to 7.3) compared to only a 29% regression (from 18.5 to 13.1) in the control group (p < 0.01). This is particularly significant in the context of evaluating infants' quality of life, given its dependence on the severity of cutaneous symptoms, pruritus, sleep disturbances, and irritability levels. Participants in  the  experimental  group  exhibited  improved  general  wellbeing, fewer nocturnal awakenings, and reduced distress, as recorded in parental questionnaires.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Results
Page: 4
Block type: text

At the initial observation stage, both groups had comparable  levels  of  total  IgE,  specific  IgE  to  food  and inhalant allergens, as well as the concentrations of key proinflammatory  cytokines  (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13),  which  confirms the  presence  of  pronounced  allergic  inflammation  in  all participants. The concentrations of IL-4 and IL-13, as typical mediators of the Th2 response, exceeded physiological ageadjusted values by more than twofold, while the level of IL-5 reflected eosinophilic activity. After eight weeks of treatment, the  experimental  group  exhibited  a  statistically  significant reduction  in  total  IgE  (p  <  0.05),  as  well  as  specific  IgE  to major  allergens, particularly β-lactoglobulin.  Furthermore, a  marked  decrease  in  IL-4  and  IL-13  levels  was  observed, suggesting a decline in Th2 response activity and an overall attenuation  of  allergic  inflammation.  In  the  control  group, similar  parameters  either  remained  unchanged  or  showed a

Prior to  treatment,  total  IgE  concentrations  in  most  participants exceeded age-adjusted norms by more than threefold, confirming  pronounced  activation  of  the  humoral  immune response, which is characteristic of atopic dermatitis. Analysis  of  the  dynamics  in  POEM  and  VAS  scores, which  reflect  the  subjective  perception  of  disease  severity and the impact of symptoms on the infants' daily activities, revealed significant differences between the two therapeutic approaches. At baseline, both parameters were elevated in both groups: the mean POEM score exceeded 18, indicating a substantial disease burden on quality of life, and the VAS itch score, as rated by the parents or caregivers, ranged between 7-8, reflecting pronounced discomfort in the infants. Participants in the experimental group demonstrated reduced frequency of night awakenings, crying, and  irritability,  as  confirmed  by  parental  questionnaires.

However,  methodological  and  interpretive  issues  must be  addressed.  The  trial  was  randomised;  however,  topical preparations, as well as the synbiotic's administration route, made blinding  participants  and  their  caregivers  impossible. In subjective outcome measures like VAS and POEM, which relied on parental reporting, this may have added performance or  observer  bias.  Parental  compliance  with  topical  or  oral medication may  have affected adherence and efficacy between groups. To reduce bias, future studies should use blinded  outcome  assessment  or  digital  therapy  adherence monitoring. Environmental and behavioural factors like nutrition,  humidity,  and  parental  skin  care  may  also  explain group variations. Both groups got the same hygiene and food advice, but its  execution  may have affected clinical results. Even if randomisation balanced these effects, residual confounding  remains.  A  brief  eight-week  follow-up  period is  another  drawback. Gains in immunological and microbial normalisation were only measured in the short term, making their permanence  questionable.  Long-term  investigations are needed to evaluate whether the decreases in IgE, IL-4, and  IL-13  indicate  temporary  regulation  or  immunological reprogramming.

In summary, the data from this study support the conclusion that the combined therapeutic approach is highly effective in treating atopic dermatitis in infants with pronounced allergic sensitisation.  The obtained results demonstrate clinical, immunological, and microbiome improvements, indicating the systemic impact of the proposed protocol. The reduction in IgE  levels,  normalisation  of  the  cytokine  profile,  decreased sensitisation, and  restoration of microbiota confirm that further investigation and implementation of such therapy will be necessary in clinical practice. Promising future directions include assessing the long-term sustainability of the achieved effect,  optimising  the  synbiotic  composition,  and  expanding the inclusion age range.

Document: 002_test
Source: resources\002_test.pdf
Section: Conclusions
Page: 9
Block type: text

The present study demonstrated the clinical and immunological efficacy of combined therapy in infants with atopic dermatitis complicated by polyvalent allergic sensitisation. By the fourth week, the experimental group showed marked improvement in cutaneous symptoms, pruritus, and quality of life, and by the  eighth  week,  reductions  in  SCORAD,  VAS,  and  POEM exceeded 60%, compared to the merely moderate progress in the control group.
Combined therapy significantly reduced total and specific IgE, including IgE to β-lactoglobulin, and decreased concentrations  of  Th2-related  cytokines  (IL-4  and  IL-13), indicating  suppression  of  allergic  inflammation  and  partial immune regulation. TNF-α levels remained stable, supporting the localised nature of the inflammatory process.  The proportion  of  children  with  severe  polyvalent  sensitisation declined  by  20%,  and  the  mean  ALEX²  class  decreased notably, reflecting a shift toward a less-reactive allergic profile.
Microbiome  assessment  revealed  favourable  changes consistent  with  restoration  of  microbial  balance,  including greater Bifidobacterium spp. abundance and improved diversity  in  the  microbiome.  This  supports  the  idea  of  gutimmune interactions being involved in disease modulation.

Section: Conclusions
Page: 9
Block type: text

Overall,  these  findings  support  combined  therapy  as  a pathogenetically justified and effective approach to managing infantile atopic dermatitis with complex allergic sensitisation. Further  studies  with  extended  follow-up  are  warranted  to evaluate  the  durability  of  these  clinical  and  immunological improvements and their potential role in promoting allergen tolerance.
